comments.author: Yuri Z.
comments.text:
Implementation commit: 8a885dad Add Contabo deployment automation

What landed:
- Added .github/workflows/deploy-contabo.yml to build the Wave image, ship a release bundle over SSH, and run the host deploy script.
- Added deploy/contabo/{compose.yml,Caddyfile,application.conf,deploy.sh} for the Docker Compose rollout, Caddy reverse proxy, runtime config, and rollback path.
- Added docs/deployment/contabo.md to document the topology, secrets, rollout, and rollback assumptions.

Verification:
- bash -n deploy/contabo/deploy.sh
- ruby -e 'require "yaml"; YAML.load_file(".github/workflows/deploy-contabo.yml") ; puts "workflow-yaml-ok"'
- DEPLOY_ROOT=/tmp/supawave WAVE_IMAGE=test-image CANONICAL_HOST=wave.supawave.ai ROOT_HOST=supawave.ai WWW_HOST=www.supawave.ai WAVE_INTERNAL_PORT=9898 docker compose -f deploy/contabo/compose.yml config
- docker run --rm -e CANONICAL_HOST=wave.supawave.ai -e ROOT_HOST=supawave.ai -e WWW_HOST=www.supawave.ai -e WAVE_INTERNAL_PORT=9898 -v /Users/vega/devroot/worktrees/incubator-wave/deploy-supawave-contabo/deploy/contabo/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile:ro caddy:2.8.4-alpine caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --adapter caddyfile

Topology rationale:
- Docker Compose on the Contabo host is the narrowest practical runtime because Docker is already present, nginx/cloudflared are absent, and the release bundle can stay self-contained.
- Caddy on the host side keeps HTTPS and apex/www redirects simple while Wave stays internal to the compose network.
- The deployment stays SSH-driven from GitHub Actions, so rollback is just a release pointer flip plus compose restart.

## Goal
Create one canonical resume document, retire stale docs, initialize repo-local Beads tracking, and record the next-phase roadmap in epics and tasks.

## Issue
The Wave codebase already completed the Java 17, Gradle 8, GWT 2.x baseline, and Jakarta / Jetty 12 migration work, but the post-Jakarta modernization backlog is still open.

## Goal
Finish the remaining modernization work so the project has one consistent build/config story, a complete MongoDB v4 migration path, verified packaging, and a clear post-GWT roadmap.

- Config hygiene and flag consolidation.
- MongoDB v4 delta-store completion.
- Remaining library upgrade work.
- SBT parity and packaging / DX verification.
- J2CL / GWT 3 inventory and planning.

- The detailed ledger in `docs/modernization-plan.md` matches the live state of the codebase.
- Follow-on product work can proceed without re-opening the infrastructure baseline.

- MongoDB driver v4 path includes `Mongo4DeltaStore`.

- A J2CL / GWT 3 inventory exists.

title: Audit production persistence topology and multi-instance blockers
description: Map the current mutable-state topology across file, memory, and Mongo-backed stores; identify what is already Mongo-capable, what remains node-local on the active Jakarta runtime, and what still blocks safe multi-instance or blue-green deployment.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Worktree: /Users/vega/devroot/worktrees/incubator-wave/persistence-audit
Branch: persistence-audit

Audit scope:
- identify which mutable stores are file/disk, memory, Mongo v2, or Mongo v4 on the active runtime
- determine whether sessions and search are still node-local even after Mongo-backed core stores are available
- decide whether Mongo is the realistic next production persistence path versus building a standalone Wave-specific storage service

Initial findings:
- Mongo4DeltaStore and the rest of the Mongo4 store set already exist in code; the previous resume guide note saying Mongo4DeltaStore was missing was stale.
- The active Jakarta ServerModule currently provides a minimal SessionHandler instead of the legacy FileSessionDataStore-backed session persistence, making sessions a separate multi-instance blocker.
- The default search path is still `memory`, and the Lucene alternative is also local-disk based, so search consistency remains node-local even if the core stores move to Mongo.
- Recommendation from this audit is to finish Mongo as the production store path first and treat any standalone WaveDB extraction as a later architectural option, not the next move.

title: Epic: Finish the imported Wiab renderer and fragments work
description:
## Issue
The main `incubator-wave` tree already contains most of the Wiab renderer, quasi-deletion, and fragments scaffolding, but it has not yet been fully validated or finished.

## Goal
Close the remaining gaps in the already-imported Wiab core work so the dynamic renderer and fragments path are clearly supported and testable.

## Scope
- Combined feature verification.
- Remaining public renderer entrypoints.
- Fragment transport and applier behavior.
- Blocks / segment-state follow-up decisions.

## DoD
- The combined renderer + fragments + quasi-deletion path is verified on the current branch.
- Remaining TODO-level code paths are resolved or explicitly documented.
- The canonical docs and Beads backlog accurately describe the supported transport path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Dynamic renderer, fragments, and quasi-deletion can be exercised together without relying on stale merge notes.
- `DynamicRendererImpl` no longer exposes placeholder entrypoints.
- The HTTP-vs-stream fragment path is explicitly supported and documented.
- The follow-up relationship between fragment fetch and deeper blocks adoption is documented.

comments.author: Claude Opus 4.6
comments.text:
Decision analysis complete. Key findings:

1. Snapshot gating deferred indefinitely: the server always sends full WaveletSnapshot on open; no server-side skip logic exists and implementing it would require cross-cutting changes to ClientFrontendImpl, ViewChannelImpl, concurrency control, and the renderer bootstrap -- all for a problem (large-wavelet open latency) that does not exist at current deployment scale.

2. Wiab.pro blocks layer not adopted: the reference implementation is ~10,400 lines across 80+ files with a custom protobuf schema, block I/O, segment caching, and delta indexing. The current compat shim (~120 lines each for SegmentWaveletStateCompat and StorageSegmentWaveletState) derives pseudo-intervals from existing snapshots and is sufficient for fragment pipeline viewport selection and observability.

3. Existing scaffold flags (preferSegmentState, enableStorageSegmentState) retained as-is for future optionality but remain off by default.

4. forceClientFragments reclassified as dev/debug flag -- it forces applier enablement but does not gate snapshots.

Future triggers for revisiting: wavelets routinely exceeding 100 blips, per-version history navigation as a product feature, or storage growth beyond practical bounds.

Decision document: docs/snapshot-gating-decision.md

title: Fix unread counts for unopened shared waves
description:
## Issue
When one participant adds a new blip to a shared wave, another participant may not see the unread count reflected in the left messages panel until the wave is opened. That makes unopened-wave digests incorrect and hides unread work.

## Goal
Make the unopened left-panel digest compute unread counts correctly for shared waves, using the same persisted read-state model that should survive reloads.

## Plan
1. Verify the shared-wave unread reproduction against the server-side search/digest path.
2. Add a regression test around the search/digest flow for shared waves with persisted user-data read state.
3. Fix the narrowest root cause in the unopened-wave unread pipeline.
4. Re-run focused tests and document the result in Beads comments.

## DoD
- Shared waves show correct unread counts in the left panel before being opened.
- Reload preserves the same unread result.
- A regression test covers the server/client path chosen for the fix.
close_reason: Fixed in f8a34aab and covered by focused regression tests

title: Epic: Evaluate and import remaining Wiab-only product features
description:
## Issue
A large part of the core renderer and transport work was imported, but several recognizable Wiab product features never made it into the active Apache Wave tree.

## Goal
Decide which of the remaining Wiab features should be imported, in what order, and under what acceptance criteria.

## Scope
- Tags, archive, and stored searches.
- Draft mode.
- Contacts.
- The deeper snapshot / history / blocks data layer.

## DoD
- The remaining Wiab-only features are either scheduled with explicit tasks or intentionally rejected with written rationale.
- Product-facing imports happen after the underlying core renderer / fragments path is verified.
acceptance_criteria:
- Each major remaining Wiab feature has an explicit audit and go/no-go decision.
- Product features do not reopen infrastructure assumptions that should be solved in the core epic.
- The backlog makes the difference between product UX imports and deep data-layer imports explicit.

title: Audit tags, archive, and stored-search features for import
description: Compare the remaining user-facing tags/archive/search functionality in `Wiab.pro` against the current Apache Wave tree and define the minimum viable import sequence.

title: Evaluate and port draft mode
description: Assess the Wiab draft-mode workflow and determine whether it can be imported cleanly into the current editor and wavepanel stack.

title: Evaluate and port contacts storage and UI
description: Assess the Wiab contacts feature set end-to-end, including stores, RPC endpoints, and UI integration, before deciding on the import scope.

title: Make a go/no-go decision on the deeper Wiab data layer
description: Decide whether the snapshot/history/blocks storage model from `Wiab.pro` should be adopted, partially mined for ideas, or explicitly left out of scope.
close_reason: No-go on full blocks port. Compat shim sufficient. Resolved by incubator-wave-wiab-core.4 decision; see docs/snapshot-gating-decision.md. PR #73.
